title: Staff+ Day in the Life at FAANG
description: What a typical day looks like for L6/L7 engineers at FAANG, which is very different from an SAP architect's day. Covers calendar anatomy, meeting patterns, coding vs design vs people time allocation, typical weeks at Google vs Meta vs Amazon, and specific guidance for SAP engineers transitioning to the Staff+ role.

sections.title: The Staff+ Calendar — What 40 Hours Actually Looks Like

description.value:
An SAP architect's calendar is structured around formal project phases: blueprint workshops, design reviews, build sprints, test cycles, go-live planning. Meetings are scheduled weeks in advance and follow a predictable pattern. A FAANG Staff+ calendar looks very different: fragmented, reactive, and self-directed.

Typical time allocation for FAANG Staff+: 30% meetings (1:1s with mentees and skip-levels, design reviews, cross-team syncs, architecture review boards, interview loops). 30% deep work (coding complex components, writing design docs, code review, system design). 20% communication (writing async updates, Slack discussions, email, documentation, performance reviews). 20% unstructured (incident response, mentoring, learning, hallway conversations, thinking time).

SAP contrast: SAP architects spend more time in formal, scheduled meetings (project steering committees, status updates, design workshops) and less time coding. FAANG Staff+ code more but in shorter blocks — 2-3 hour deep work sessions rather than full days of uninterrupted coding. SAP architects have more predictable calendars; FAANG Staff+ have more volatile calendars with frequent interruptions (incidents, urgent discussions, pings from cross-team partners).

The most important calendar skill: Blocking focus time. If you don't block 3-4 hours daily for deep work, your calendar will fill with meetings. SAP engineers, accustomed to having their calendars managed by project coordinators, often fail to protect their focus time. At FAANG, your calendar is your responsibility. Block it early, defend it ruthlessly.

sections.title: A Typical Week at Google L6

description.value:
Monday: 9:00-10:00 Design review (reviewing a team member's design doc for a new storage service). 10:00-11:00 Team sync (weekly updates, prioritization for the sprint). 11:00-12:00 1:1 with skip-level (Director of Engineering, discussing your team's quarterly OKRs and technical strategy). 12:00-13:00 Lunch. 13:00-16:00 Deep work block (coding a complex distributed locking mechanism for the team's new service). 16:00-17:00 1:1 with two mentees (30 min each, career development and design feedback).

Tuesday: 9:00-10:00 Cross-team sync for migration project (coordinating with 3 teams on the API migration). 10:00-12:00 Deep work block (writing a design doc for the next quarter's roadmap initiative). 12:00-13:00 Lunch. 13:00-14:00 Architecture review board (reviewing another team's architecture proposal). 14:00-16:00 Coding block (reviewing 5 diffs from team members, writing code for the caching layer). 16:00-17:00 Interview (conducting a system design interview for an L5 candidate).

Wednesday: 9:00-09:30 On-call handoff (if on rotation, reviewing incidents from last week). 09:30-10:30 Design review (team member's API design). 10:30-12:00 Deep work block (coding). 12:00-13:00 Lunch. 13:00-16:00 Unstructured afternoon (this week: prototyping a new approach to data replication, answering Slack questions from the team). 16:00-17:00 1:1 with manager (weekly sync: priorities, challenges, career growth).

Thursday: 9:00-11:00 Deep work block (no meetings morning — focus on the critical path for the quarter). 11:00-12:00 Design review. 12:00-13:00 Lunch and learn (team member presents their project). 13:00-15:00 Code review and mentoring (pair programming with a junior engineer on a tricky concurrency issue). 15:00-16:00 Update design doc based on feedback. 16:00-17:00 Prep for next week's all-hands demo.

Friday: No meeting day (Google culture: Friday is for deep work). 9:00-12:00 Write the technical vision doc for the next half. 12:00-13:00 Lunch. 13:00-15:00 Catch up on code review backlog, write a tech note for the team wiki. 15:00-17:00 Learning time (reading papers, exploring a new technology, contributing to open source).

sections.title: A Typical Week at Amazon L6 vs Meta E6

description.value:
Amazon L6 week: Amazon's culture emphasizes writing and thoroughness, so expect more time spent on written documents. Monday: weekly business review (review team metrics, customer feedback, operational health). Tuesday: writing narratives for upcoming OP1/OP2 planning (Amazon's annual planning process requires 6-pagers). Wednesday: design review and coding — Amazon Staff+ engineers write more code than Google Staff+ because Amazon maintains a 'builder' culture. Thursday: interview rounds (Amazon expects all engineers to interview, Staff+ more so). Friday: on-call (if on rotation) or deep work on architecture. Amazon weeks have more writing and less unstructured exploration than Google.

Meta E6 week: Meta's culture emphasizes speed and execution. Monday: sprint planning (Meta uses half-week sprints), 1:1s, coding blocks. Tuesday: cross-functional with PM and designers (Meta is design-led), heavy coding. Wednesday: engineering all-hands, code review, performance review writing (during calibration season). Thursday: on-call or design review, hackathon projects (Meta encourages 10% time). Friday: demos, tech talks, lighter day. Meta weeks are more fast-paced, with shorter feedback loops and more emphasis on shipping than analysis.

Key differences across companies: Google has more unstructured time ('20% time' culture) and more design review rigor. Amazon has more writing (narratives, 6-pagers for planning) and more emphasis on operational metrics. Meta has more coding and faster shipping cycles with less formal process. Apple has more secrecy and deeper focus on individual contribution — fewer meetings and more uninterrupted work time. Choose the company that matches your preferred working style.

sections.title: Meetings — Which Ones Matter and Which to Decline

description.value:
Meeting culture differs dramatically between SAP and FAANG. SAP projects have many formal meetings (steering committees, project board reviews, status meetings with every stakeholder). FAANG has fewer but more focused meetings. Learning which meetings to attend and which to decline is a critical skill.

MUST attend: Design reviews (where architecture decisions are made — missing these means decisions are made without you). 1:1s with your manager and skip-level (visibility, alignment, career growth). Incident postmortems (learning from failures, building operational culture). Team syncs (alignment on priorities, understanding what your team is working on).

SHOULD attend: Cross-team syncs (building relationships, understanding dependencies). Architecture review board (technical leadership, influencing org-wide decisions). Interview loops (giving back to the community, calibrating your own evaluation skills). All-hands (company/org-level context, though you can watch the recording).

DECLINE: Status-only meetings where you could read the update in a doc. Large meetings where you're included 'for awareness' (ask to be removed). Meetings without agendas (ask for one before accepting). Recurring meetings that no longer serve a purpose (cancel them proactively).

SAP-specific meeting advice: In SAP, attendance is often mandatory and you're expected to 'represent' your workstream. At FAANG, your time is your responsibility. If a meeting doesn't need you, don't go. If a meeting could be a Slack message, suggest that instead. FAANG respects people who protect their focus time — declining a meeting with a clear reason ('I'm focusing on the Q3 migration design this week, can I review the meeting notes async?') is seen as mature prioritization, not disengagement.

decisionTree.title: Should You Attend This Meeting?
decisionTree.prompt: Your calendar has a recurring meeting: 'Cross-team Architecture Sync - Weekly.' It's been running for 3 months, and you've attended every week. You notice that the last 3 meetings had no decisions made and no action items for you. Should you continue attending?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Is a decision being made or is information being shared?" If decisions are being made that affect your team or your work, attend. If it's purely information sharing (status updates, demos), ask for the summary document and decline.

branches.condition: Decisions affecting your work are made in this meeting
branches.path: ATTEND. But ask the organizer to put decisions first on the agenda so you can leave after the decision is made. Also ask for a decision log so you don't need to attend to know what was decided.

branches.condition: Information is shared that you can read later
branches.path: DECLINE. Ask the organizer to share meeting notes or a summary document. Offer to review the doc async and provide feedback. If no one objects to you missing, the meeting didn't need you.

branches.condition: You're attending 'for visibility' or 'just in case'
branches.path: DECLINE. 'Just in case' attendance is the biggest time sink at FAANG. If something urgent comes up that requires you, someone will ping you. The odds that a critical issue surfaces in the 5 minutes you're NOT in the meeting are near zero.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The best meeting filter: ask yourself 'If I skip this meeting, will anyone notice?' If the answer is no, you shouldn't have been there. If the answer is yes, ask why. You might be making key decisions or blocking others — those meetings matter. But if the answer is yes because 'my input is occasionally needed,' suggest an async review instead.

description.value:
One of the most common questions from SAP engineers: 'Will I still code at Staff+ level?' The answer is yes, but differently than at L4/L5. An SAP architect often stops coding entirely and shifts to design and governance. A FAANG Staff+ engineer continues coding, but the nature of the coding changes.

What Staff+ coding looks like: You write less code than L4/L5 engineers (maybe 20-30% of your time instead of 60-70%). Your code focuses on: (1) Complex components that require deep system thinking (distributed concurrency, fault-tolerant patterns, performance-critical paths). (2) Architectural migrations and refactoring (breaking a monolith into services, migrating databases, changing core abstractions). (3) Prototypes and proofs-of-concept (validating an approach before the team commits to it). You also spend significant time on code review — not just checking for bugs, but mentoring through code, setting coding standards, and ensuring architectural consistency across the codebase.

Code review at Staff+ level: This is where you have the most impact through code. You review 5-10 diffs per week, and each review is an opportunity to: teach design patterns, catch architectural issues before they compound, and set the quality standard for the team. Your reviews should be thorough but kind: 'This approach works, but consider this edge case...' rather than 'This is wrong.'

SAP-specific coding advice: Your ABAP experience gives you strong fundamentals in: transaction management, data consistency, error handling, and modular design. These patterns transfer directly to any language. However, you need to learn the specific coding conventions of your FAANG company: Google's C++/Java style guide, Meta's Hack/Python idioms, Amazon's Java conventions. Invest your first 3 months in learning the coding patterns of your new team, not in trying to be the fastest coder.

sections.title: The Unwritten Rules of Staff+ Daily Life

description.value:
Beyond the calendar and the meetings, there are unwritten rules that SAP engineers discover (sometimes painfully) in their first year at FAANG. These rules aren't documented anywhere, but violating them will slow your career growth.

Rule 1: Your calendar is public. At FAANG, everyone can see everyone's calendar. If your calendar has empty blocks, someone will fill them. Block focus time proactively: 'Coding Block — Do Not Disturb.' If you don't, you'll end up in 8 hours of meetings every day.

Rule 2: Async first. Before scheduling a meeting, write a doc. Before asking a question in Slack, search for the answer. Before escalating, send a summary. FAANG values written communication because it scales. If you default to meetings, you'll be seen as someone who doesn't respect others' time.

Rule 3: Respond within 1 hour during working hours. Slack messages and emails should be acknowledged within an hour (even if the answer is 'I'll look into this and respond by Friday'). FAANG moves fast — if you're unresponsive for a day, people assume you're blocked or disengaged.

Rule 4: You don't have direct reports, but you have influence. Staff+ engineers at FAANG are individual contributors — you don't manage anyone. But you influence the technical direction of your org. This is a hard adjustment from SAP, where titles like 'Solution Architect' come with governance authority. At FAANG, your influence comes from technical credibility, not position. Build it through good decisions, clear writing, and consistent mentorship.

Rule 5: Your most important work is often invisible. The design doc you wrote that prevented a bad architecture decision. The mentoring conversation that helped an L5 get promoted to L6. The code review that caught a production bug before it shipped. These things don't show up on a sprint burndown chart. But they're what Staff+ is paid for. Don't expect applause for them — but do document them for your performance review.

Rule 6: Protect your energy, not just your time. A FAANG Staff+ week is mentally demanding. You're making high-stakes decisions, mediating conflicts, writing important documents, and mentoring multiple people. You can't do this well for 50 hours every week. Protect your evenings and weekends. Take real vacations. The most productive Staff+ engineers are not the ones working the most hours — they're the ones who are most effective during the hours they work.

adversarialResponse.title: Handling the 'I Was an Architect — Now I'm an IC' Identity Shift
adversarialResponse.challenge: You were a respected Solution Architect in SAP, leading teams, making final technical decisions, and reporting to VPs. At FAANG, you're a Staff IC with no direct reports, no governance authority, and less organizational status. Your ego struggles with this. Your family asks: 'Didn't you get a promotion?'
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Try to recreate your SAP authority structure at FAANG. Ask for direct reports. Demand governance authority. Refer to your previous title. Complain that FAANG doesn't value your experience. This will damage your reputation and slow your growth.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Recognize that FAANG Staff+ is NOT a demotion — it's a different kind of leadership. Your influence comes from technical credibility, not organizational authority. The freedom is liberating: you spend your time on technical decisions, not personnel management. You own your calendar. You choose what to work on. You mentor without the baggage of being a manager. Many SAP architects who transition to FAANG Staff+ find that they love being an IC — they just needed to get over the identity shift. Give it 6 months before judging the new role.
adversarialResponse.pattern: The 'different kind of leadership' reframe: At SAP, leadership means authority (approving designs, assigning work). At FAANG, leadership means influence (convincing through reasoning, mentoring through code review, setting technical direction through design docs). Both are leadership — but one depends on position, the other depends on skill. The latter is harder to earn but more rewarding once you have it.

details: Staff+ Insight: The biggest calendar shock for SAP engineers is the lack of structure. SAP projects have rigid phases and predictable schedules. FAANG has none of that — your week is what you make it. The most successful SAP-to-FAANG transitions are engineers who aggressively protect their deep work time and don't let meetings consume their calendar. Block 3-4 hours of focus time daily, treat it as non-negotiable, and measure your output by decisions influenced and designs created, not by hours spent in meetings. Also: the identity shift from 'Solution Architect' (authority figure) to 'Staff+ IC' (influencer without authority) is the hardest psychological adjustment. Give it at least 6 months before judging whether the new role suits you.
